item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Apart from that, not even in 2009 this should have been the norm, if you check around.

item.title: Image credit
item.content:encoded:
The image used to illustrate SCP-108 is taken from https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:U10_Innsbrucker_Platz.jpg

and is available for use under Creative Commons

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very late, but the choice of the person involved contrasts with the bunker. It reinforces that the makers of the bunker probably didn't intend this, and adds to the sense of weird happenstance throughout the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The subject was recovered without incident by Task Force Epsilon-9 disguised as High Risk Warrant Team officers in a pre-dawn raid in June of 19██. By the end of the cleanup operation the GP, ENT, 11 hospital personnel and two civilians were terminated.

These two sentences feel very dated. The Foundation of today would probably just arrange for the subject's transfer, and barring that, I find it hard to imagine a scenario in which a military task force is required to terminate 15 people.
Even if this all did occur, 15 deaths is hardly "without incident," even for the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"black people's nostrils are SO BIG they may as well be a void to another world!!!!"

Of course, you can interpret SCP articles however you want, but I think this is a bit of a stretch. Is it not more likely that they picked a black woman in order to stand out against the crowd of white dudes on the wiki at the time? Or that they just felt like picking a black woman? There's no reason to immediately jump to racial stereotypes.

As an aside, 'subtle yet very prominent tone' doesn't make any sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The wandering is fine and all, whatever. More potentially harmful SCPs have been allowed freedom. However, the mass (unnecessary and completely avoidable) casualties strike me as negligent, and honestly… The fact that the author went with an African-American woman with a portal in her nostrils seems like a very, very explicit jab at the general facial structure of african/african-american people at large. I mean, really. Did nobody else consider that this author was mocking the tendency for larger nostrils in african-american people/people with african heritage when reading this? I feel as if there is a subtle yet very prominent tone of "black people's nostrils are SO BIG they may as well be a void to another world!!!!" sort of implication here. Either way, downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The SCP article could've been written on paper, and then transcribed onto the internet after its invention due to convenience. Responding to old post because paradoxes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this SCP, the character is diverse and I love the creative way of torture or suffering the character was put through (having a nazi bunker in your nose is gonna fucking suck) and I love big places in small areas.

Just a neat story, tone could use a lot of work tho, and the mass killing kinda was a bit screwed up.

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The wandering seems fine to me, considering she's just a woman whose nose is apparently a portal, and the death, while excessive, is fine by me. Plenty of good things have unnecessary death. Maybe I'm just a little twisted, but 13 deaths seems like a weird sole reason to downvote. Tell me when the deaths hit upward of 20, then we'll talk.
Also,

The original galvanized-iron nail has yet to be found.

Beautiful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm actually okay with the wandering. The mass death still warrants an insta-downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not necessarily. It could be set in the future.

Responding to old post because fractals.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's at +200. It's not getting rewritten.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps you should suggest a rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This disappoints me greatly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And now it's back to 200.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The filtration filling must be removed on a regular basis when it gets expended (roughly once a week) as the subject is essentially inhaling the atmosphere of a formerly-sealed Nazi mass war grave.

Okay, this does break tone pretty hard, but… goddamn if it doesn't make the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't even re-read the whole thing. The free-wandering was just a downvote-trigger red flag, one that your comment a wee bit upthread tells me wouldn't have gone away had I stopped to re-read the whole thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You downvoted for an anomalous person that has no living things in her wandering around, and not the part where the Foundation killed people in a hospital to recover her just because?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read this before I had an account, and I just rediscovered and recognized it.

I hate to take it's +200 away, but the bit about "free wandering" is a downvote from me, and I'm the guy who came up with a classification system for whether or not a skip can do that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoting for lax containment (if she's conditioned to accept the Foundation then why does she need wandering priveledges?) and for unnecessary civilian casualties. I'm fine with them /happening/, but give us an explanation for it; did someone notice something wrong with your papers and alert security before they could be neutralized?

I loved the rest of the article, though. As odd (and silly) as the premise is, it's executed well enough that it still felt good to read it. And I loved the little implications that there might be something in there, still (the fact that the nail isn't there anymore is the biggest one; the cannibalism could have been incidental).

As for the location of the base, I see two possibilities: either this is extradimensional, and was going to be brought back into reality somewhere tactically advantageous when they had enough planes to be a serious threat, or it still exists somewhere. There were those hangar doors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The subject was recovered without incident by Task Force Epsilon-9 disguised as High Risk Warrant Team officers in a pre-dawn raid in June of 19██. By the end of the cleanup operation the GP, ENT, 11 hospital personnel and two civilians were terminated.

Holy SHIT is this excessive. No matter how good the concept is, I have to downvote with this. That is just… wow.

item.title: Agree 100 percent
item.content:encoded: I agree completely. Downvoting this for the unnecessary civilian casualties.

item.title: Downvoted, sorry but. . .
item.content:encoded: Downvoted for the unnecessary civilian deaths. The Foundation DOES have very effective amnestics, you know…they don't go around just killing innocent civilians if they don't have to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has not aged well. First, she can wander about as she likes? Sure, give her minimal containment with access to an enclosed courtyard or something, but the rest of the site? Especially where there's a huge risk of literal lunch room chatter? Not a thing anymore.

And second, seriously, you killed 13 people when posing as a specialist and taking her to a different clinic would've worked fine? Forget amnesiacs, simple social engineering would work here — look like a doctor, act like a doctor, wheel a patient out the door for a transfer to a specialized facility. Simple, and a lot less fishy than shooting the place up. (Particularly since, if I follow the timeline here, all they knew was that her nose didn't act like noses are supposed to, gonna kill 13 people over that? That's a lolFoundation level screwup.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But there exists the possibility that if you remove the nose, maybe the portal stops working. It's not a risk they're willing to take, especially because it's entirely unnecessary, especially for the time being. When health complications become a problem it may be an option they'll consider.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry that my first comment on site is to an old post, but I had to.

I agree with you guys, and something's really bugging me. How come the Foundation didn't think of simply cutting off the nose? Yes we don't destroy, we protect, we aren't evil but just cold. But the SCP in question isn't the woman, the woman is the host and she can obviously live without her nose. Instead of killing all those civilians and imprisoning her, cutting her nose off and preserving it for further study, while giving amnesiacs to her and the civilians, stating that she was in an accident which caused her to lose her nose seems much more reasonable to me. You wouldn't have to worry about the woman's consent either, and forcing a Micro SD through a "dead" and "removed" nose is much more humane than forcing it up a living person's nose.

PS: I've read almost all the guides in the guides hub, and about 120 SCP's, and I still think my version fits the foundation approach…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This really bothered me as well. Terminating civilians when necessary for the greater good is acceptable, if unfortunate. But whacking a baker's dozen to protect the "secret of the Nazi nose bunker" seems a wee bit bat-shit insane, especially given the other available options. I would up-vote if not for the wanton massacre. The rest of the article is excellent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep, sounds completely unneccessary from outside perspective, and as incredible fuck-up in-universe. HOW you can screw things that badly? It seems added for "add some dead bodies from hand of ruthless Foundation just because, yay". Dumb, simply dumb.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why were 13 people murdered? Couldn't they have just told her that they might have a cure? Then they could have just walked out of there with her.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but as I'm sure you used to I have to ask. Why did the hospital staff and civilians have to die when a cover story of recovery, or amnesiacs, would suffice?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have long liked this one, but apparently had not remembered to upvote until now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoting due to the sheer creativity without it being over-the-top or really stupid. Nazi bunker in a woman's nose? I never woulda thought of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It would be very creepy if something were moving around in that bunker. I wonder if it's even accessible from the "real" world. That for some reason the bunker is just gone from our world and only exists in some limbo-like realm that exists through this woman's nostril.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A WW-2 nazi bunker in a human nasal cavity, i never would've thought of that! +1 from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This might be my favorite part of this article. The fact that his bunker can't be properly explored - they can't even confirm whether it's an alternative dimension or some place in our Earth, or whether it is in our present time or not - adds a lot to the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this article, but those containment procedures really rub me the wrong way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like SCPs that are odd locations which can't be reached by a human team, like that tiny portal to an alternate Earth. It helps retain the mystery if you can't send countless numbers of D-class and MTF agents to investigate and die horribly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a chick with a nazi bunker in her nose.

I have never wanted to upnvote so hard in my life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a chick with a nazi bunker in her nose.

I have never wanted to downvote so hard in my life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup. I wasn't going to vote, but that last line did it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I always assumed that since we're reading this on a modern website/"intranet", it was set in modern times.

And I'm only responding to your old post because you did so with the previous, so…exceptions?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This may be the damn strangest thing I have read on this site, which is saying something. I love it, especially that last line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … that's the noise she would make, yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Foundation hasn't always had amnesiacs, and they've (non-canon) absorbed other groups that may not have had them at the time.

Plus, sometimes "Things go badly but according to plan"

Actually, the interesting things is that as much as I like the concept, the picture doesn't match the text, and I don't think it's very well explored. Maybe that's why I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot more than seems rational. How the hell did the author come up with this…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hear, hear.
We don't have amnesiacs anymore. We don't even have scrubs to masquerade as medics and move her to "another clinic". But we do have guns.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
By the end of the cleanup operation the GP, ENT, 11 hospital personnel and two civilians were terminated

Good Lord

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm still kind of surprised no one has suggested an exploratory mission using SCP-786 and a micro blimp…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Remember, she can't be evacuated through her own nose.

snerk

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
#4a - I'm wondering if it's even the same time - after, if it's portal through space, why not through time? Heck, it could all be contained in SCP-17591
#4b - Lenin

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As stated above, I've always assumed the pneumonia was caused by portions of her respiratory system exposed to the german elements in the remote spot where the bunker used to be.

I'd also like to remind readers that just because the article says the Foundation doesn't know where the bunker is does not mean they actually do not know. I generally assume that many of the SCP articles contain disinformation designed to protect certain secrets in case of disclosure, and this is an excellent example of a situation in which that omission can be carried out without affecting research below a certain level. I imagine that the fully declassified O5 version of this report not only gives coordinates for where the bunker is/was, but methods or attempted methods of entry as well.

Remember, she can't be evacuated through her own nose. This tells us that the Foundation must have an alternate method of access to the bunker, so that after all personnel or information or whatever has gone in they'll have a way to get it back after the woman is shot in the face by whatever attackers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hence all the pneumonia.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, I'm not the author, but Gene mentioned this in the discussion thread for something I posted, so now I feel all obligated and stuff:

1) True, but rereading it, it seems like a calculated risk. That, and I don't doubt that it is regularly monitored. I can see the Foundation having tiny little defenses set up on the other side, just in case something like that happened. Gas someone, tie them down Gulliver and the Lilliputians style, begin interrogation to find out where the other side is. But, then again, it's only proposed, isn't it?

2) Interesting thought. Not to mention, she's already fifty-one. What happens if she dies in a couple of years? But, then again, the Foundation has access to things to fix that.

3) I think that's a reference to breathing the musty air of the bunker. Eventually, she was overcome by what she was breathing, which people only seemed to notice after she tried the trick (presumably also what triggered the issue to begin with).

4) Ahh, see? That makes it interesting. If it is extra-dimensional, maybe they didn't actually have the bacteria there that would have caused the breakdown? Mummification maybe, though… I'm not sure what happens to a body in this state… How did you guys preserve Lenin?

5) They didn't do that until they'd already looked inside her nose and presumably seen blackness… Curiosity? What if you found a big hole in the ground and you couldn't see the bottom? Would you drop a rock down it?

6) I stand by the fact that a doctor, faced with something they couldn't explain, did their best to explore it fully. FOR SCIENCE!

7) Well, she can when she's consciously breathing… What about when she's asleep? We could try to pinch her nose shut, but then wouldn't she just keep pulling from inside the portal itself? We'd have to rig a contraption that would block her ability to breath through her nose entirely, and… I kind of think just treating the pneumonia regularly might be easier… Eh. Who can say?

8) Related to question seven: Since she got sick from breathing the presumably damp and stale air for days, I want to assume that the portal is open on both sides. But then again, who can say?

Again, I'm not the author, and I don't have a 100% understanding of this one, but I hope I at least managed to address your concerns.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now this object raises some questions:
1) Foundation scientists are not safe to assume the bunker exists outside of our reality. Therefore it's not entirely impossible for someone to stumble upon the Foundation's pile of MicroSDs. Mass disclosure ahoy!
2) What if an accident happens to the woman? Will the portal stay after she dies?
3) Why was she diagnosed with pneumonia? Bad breath is not a specific symptom of it.
4) Nazi corpses. They should be either skeletonized or mummified, the second being a lot less likely. Even if the report wasn't written in the present, fiber optics were not invented until the 70s, and 25 years were more than enough for the corpses to decompose completely. Either way, they're not supposed to stink.
5) Pneumonia is an inflammation of lungs. Why would the doctor try to ram the endoscope through her nasopharynx, trachea and bronchial tubes all the way to her lungs (which would probably harm her) instead of doing an X-ray?
6) Again, pneumonia is not for an ENT to deal with. Nothing to do with ears, nose or throat. Why was he (or she) involved?
7) Why waste precious time and resources when she can simply breathe through her mouth?
8) Nasal cavity is connected to the throat. Did anyone try to inspect the portal that way instead of the nose?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Forget that, think about wind chill. And dust. Or all the other consequences of being exposed to the elements like that…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now I have the image of a small songbird, perched upon those six or seven inches of human respiratory system, pecking occasionally…

…Man, talk about an itch you can't scratch!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I prefer to believe that the bunker no longer exists in any location on earth, and that instead somewhere in the woods of eastern germany six or seven inches of human respiratory system hover about fifteen feet above a small valley.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Couldn't they shove a tiny GPS sensor up her nose? Given that it's an airplane hangar, it's not below ground.
